children.name: Loxosceles reclusa
children.description: Potentially necrotic venom containing sphingomyelinase D. Violin-shaped marking on cephalothorax. Bites uncommon; the spider is genuinely reclusive.

children.name: Loxosceles laeta
children.description: Most dangerous recluse; native to South America. Venom can cause systemic loxoscelism (haemolysis and organ failure) in addition to local necrosis.

children.name: Loxosceles rufescens
children.description: Main recluse in Europe and the Mediterranean. Significant envenomation rare.

children.name: Loxosceles gaucho
children.description: A major cause of envenomation in southeastern Brazil; its bite can produce severe necrotic lesions (cutaneous loxoscelism).

children.name: Loxosceles intermedia
children.description: Responsible for the majority of recluse bites in southern Brazil; thrives indoors, hiding behind furniture and in stored materials.

children.name: Loxosceles deserta
children.description: A native recluse of the deserts of the southwestern United States; lives under rocks and in rodent burrows away from human dwellings.

children.name: Loxosceles arizonica
children.description: A southwestern North American recluse of arid uplands; bites are rare and rarely produce significant necrosis.

children.name: Loxosceles apachea
children.description: A recluse of the Chihuahuan Desert region of the US and Mexico, found under rocks and debris in dry habitats.

children.name: Loxosceles devia
children.description: A recluse native to Texas and northeastern Mexico, sometimes found in buildings within its range.

children.name: Loxosceles boneti
children.description: A Mexican recluse of medical importance; lives in caves and human structures.

children.name: Loxosceles spinulosa
children.description: A recluse widespread in southern Africa, found under bark and stones; bites are infrequently reported.

children.name: Loxosceles parrami
children.description: A recluse described from southern Africa, occurring in dry rocky habitats.

children.name: Loxosceles pilosa
children.description: A South American recluse of Argentina and neighbouring regions, found in dry sheltered microhabitats.

children.name: Loxosceles amazonica
children.description: A recluse of the Amazon basin, occurring under loose bark and in leaf litter of tropical forest.

children.name: Loxosceles tenango
children.description: A cave-associated recluse from central Mexico, one of many Mexican species in this diverse genus.

children.name: Loxosceles yeso
children.description: A Mexican recluse adapted to gypsum cave systems, illustrating the genus's frequent cave-dwelling habits.

children.name: Loxosceles colombiensis
children.description: A recluse of Colombia and the northern Andes, occurring in dry valleys and increasingly inside human dwellings.

children.name: Loxosceles variegata
children.description: A South American recluse with a mottled pattern, found under rocks and bark in arid habitats of Argentina and Bolivia.

children.name: Loxosceles similis
children.description: A Brazilian recluse strongly associated with caves and rock shelters, where it preys on cave-dwelling invertebrates.

children.name: Loxosceles blanda
children.description: A small, pale recluse of the Chihuahuan Desert found in New Mexico and Mexico; generally considered less medically significant than its relatives.

children.name: Loxosceles unicolor
children.description: A uniformly pale-brown recluse of the southwestern United States and Baja California, living under rocks in desert scrub habitats.

children.name: Loxosceles martha
children.description: A North American recluse of limited range in Mexico; like most congeners it lurks under surface objects by day and hunts by night.

children.name: Loxosceles kaiba
children.description: A recluse associated with the Kaibab Plateau region of the American Southwest, found under bark and within rock crevices in ponderosa pine habitat.

children.name: Loxosceles nordestina
children.description: A recluse of northeastern Brazil's semi-arid Caatinga and coastal zones; one of the species responsible for human envenomation in the region.

children.name: Loxosceles hirsuta
children.description: A moderately bristly recluse of South America's Atlantic Forest margins; inhabits leaf litter, under bark, and occasionally buildings.

children.name: Loxosceles concolor
children.description: A uniformly-coloured South American recluse with no distinct violin mark; found under bark and in rocky crevices in Argentina and Chile.

children.name: Loxosceles anomala
children.description: A South American recluse with an atypically patterned abdomen; found in Brazil in sheltered dry microhabitats.

children.name: Loxosceles chapadensis
children.description: A recluse from Brazil's Chapada Diamantina highlands; inhabits rocky outcrops and caves in this biodiversity hotspot.

children.name: Loxosceles rufipes
children.description: A South American recluse distinguished by reddish-tinted legs; occurs in dry forest and scrub habitats of eastern South America.

children.name: Loxosceles surca
children.description: A recluse of Peru and Bolivia described from high-altitude valleys; one of the highest-elevation members of the genus.

children.name: Loxosceles spadicea
children.description: A chestnut-brown recluse of the Southern Cone of South America; typically found under rocks and bark in scrubland and dry forest.

children.name: Loxosceles misteca
children.description: A Mexican recluse from the Mixteca region of Oaxaca and Puebla; one of dozens of recluse species described from Mexico's diverse highlands.

children.name: Loxosceles yucatana
children.description: A recluse of the Yucatán Peninsula; inhabits limestone cave systems and rocky outcrops of this low-lying karst landscape.

children.name: Loxosceles olmeca
children.description: A recluse of southern Mexico's humid coastal lowlands named for the ancient Olmec culture region; found under bark and in leaf litter.

children.name: Loxosceles caribbaea
children.description: A recluse of the Caribbean basin, recorded from several islands and the surrounding mainland; favours dry rock shelters and introduced structures.

children.name: Loxosceles antillicola
children.description: A lesser-known recluse associated with the Greater Antilles; one of several island-dwelling Loxosceles with restricted distributions.

children.name: Loxosceles speluncarum
children.description: A troglophilic recluse from South American cave systems; pale and long-legged relative to surface-dwelling congeners, an adaptation to subterranean life.

children.name: Loxosceles immodesta
children.description: A South American recluse of moderate size; occupies dry coastal scrub habitats in Chile and Peru.

children.name: Loxosceles puortoi
children.description: A Brazilian recluse described by Schenkel and named in honour of arachnologist Romano Puorto; found in southeastern Brazil.

children.name: Loxosceles simillima
children.description: An African recluse closely resembling other continental species; found in southern and eastern Africa in dry bush habitats.

children.name: Loxosceles bergeri
children.description: An African recluse named after arachnologist Eugene Berger; inhabits dry, rocky habitats in southern and East Africa.

children.name: Loxosceles boliviensis
children.description: A recluse of Bolivia's dry inter-Andean valleys and chaco margins; found under rocks and debris at moderate elevations.

children.name: Loxosceles inornata
children.description: A plain, unmarked recluse of Mexico and Central America; lacks the strong violin marking seen in some relatives, making field identification tricky.

children.name: Loxosceles dimidiata
children.description: A recluse of southern Mexico and Central America with a two-toned coloration; occurs in rocky ravines and dry forest understory.

children.name: Loxosceles palaciosi
children.description: A Mexican recluse named in honour of R. Palacios; found in central and western Mexico in dry scrub and cave mouths.

children.name: Loxosceles franckei
children.description: A recluse named after arachnologist O. F. Francke; endemic to Mexico's karst cave systems in the Huasteca region.

children.name: Loxosceles piura
children.description: A recluse of northern Peru's Piura region; inhabits dry coastal scrub and modified environments such as adobe structures.

children.name: Loxosceles coylei
children.description: A recluse of Mexico named after arachnologist Frederick Coyle; found in scrubby tropical dry forest microhabitats.

children.name: Loxosceles diablo
children.description: A deep-cave recluse from the Sierra del Abra of Mexico; highly troglomorphic with elongated appendages and reduced pigmentation.

children.name: Loxosceles lutea
children.description: A pale-yellow recluse of South American dry habitats; its light colouration provides camouflage among sandy soils and pale leaf litter.

children.name: Loxosceles erythromela
children.description: A vibrantly marked recluse of Caribbean origin with reddish and dark-contrasting patterns; occasionally intercepted as an introduced species.

children.name: Loxosceles striatipes
children.description: A South American recluse distinguished by faint longitudinal stripes on its legs; inhabits dry forest and rock outcrops.

children.name: Loxosceles longipes
children.description: A South American recluse with notably long legs relative to body size; found in humid and dry transitional forest zones.

children.name: Loxosceles poderosa
children.description: One of the larger South American recluses, described from Brazil; its venom potency has been assessed as medically significant.

children.name: Loxosceles bahiensis
children.description: A recluse endemic to the state of Bahia in northeastern Brazil; found in rocky caatinga vegetation and human dwellings.

children.name: Loxosceles melloi
children.description: A Brazilian recluse named after arachnologist C. F. Mello-Leitão; inhabits dry forest and cerrado habitats of central Brazil.

children.name: Loxosceles adelaida
children.description: A small recluse from the dry coastal valleys of Peru and Chile. Its venom is under active study for potential therapeutic applications.

children.name: Loxosceles lactus
children.description: A common recluse of Chile and Peru. Its bite can cause severe necrotic lesions. Found in both urban and rural environments.

children.name: Loxosceles blagracensis
children.description: A small recluse of the southwestern US. Its venom is relatively mild compared to L. reclusa.

children.name: Loxosceles rufofemorata
children.description: A recluse from the Pampas region of Argentina, named for its red-coloured femurs. One of several Loxosceles species responsible for cases of loxoscelism in Argentina.

children.name: Loxosceles ancora
children.description: A South American recluse of the arid coastal valleys of Peru. Its specific name refers to the anchor-shaped marking on the carapace.

children.name: Loxosceles bruma
children.description: A small brown spider of the Brazilian cerrado. Its common name refers to its peak reproductive activity during the cooler months.

children.name: Loxosceles bettainensis
children.description: A recently described species from the Bettain region of Colombia. Its known distribution is limited to a small area of tropical dry forest.

children.name: Loxosceles mista
children.description: A South American recluse of the Amazon basin with variable colouration.

children.name: Loxosceles tauropluta
children.description: A large recluse of the Brazilian cerrado with a distinctive bull-like carapace marking.

children.name: Loxosceles marmorata
children.description: A recluse with marbled markings on the carapace, found in Brazil.

children.name: Loxosceles pallida
children.description: A pale recluse of the Baja California Peninsula.

children.name: Loxosceles sulcata
children.description: A recluse of the Chilean matorral with grooved carapace texture.

children.name: Loxosceles tenuis
children.description: A slender recluse of the Paraguayan Chaco.

children.name: Loxosceles trivialis
children.description: A common recluse of central Brazil found in both forests and human dwellings.

children.name: Loxosceles varia
children.description: A variable recluse of the Brazilian Amazon with diverse colour morphs.

children.name: Sicarius andreeae
children.description: A sand spider of the Peruvian coastal deserts.

children.name: Sicarius boliviensis
children.description: A sand spider of the Bolivian dry valleys.

children.name: Sicarius chilensis
children.description: A sand spider of Chile's Atacama region.

children.name: Sicarius hahni
children.description: Ambush hunter of southern African deserts; buries under sand with only eyes exposed. Highly cytotoxic venom; at least two documented human fatalities though bites extremely rare.

children.name: Sicarius thomisoides
children.description: A flattened, crab-like ambush predator of the Atacama and other South American deserts; buries in sand and can survive many months without food.

children.name: Sicarius ornatus
children.description: A Brazilian six-eyed sand spider with highly cytotoxic, sphingomyelinase-rich venom; buries beneath sand in arid habitats.

children.name: Sicarius tropicus
children.description: A six-eyed sand spider of dry South American regions; an ambush hunter that flicks sand over its body for camouflage.

children.name: Sicarius rupestris
children.description: A South American sand spider associated with rocky desert outcrops; like its relatives it is extremely tolerant of starvation and dehydration.

children.name: Sicarius cariri
children.description: A six-eyed sand spider described from the semi-arid Caatinga of northeastern Brazil.

children.name: Sicarius gracilis
children.description: A slender-legged South American sand spider of Andean foothills; its flattened body allows it to squeeze beneath flat stones and root mats.

children.name: Sicarius peruensis
children.description: A six-eyed sand spider from Peru's coastal and inter-Andean dry zones; uses a sit-and-wait hunting strategy in sandy substrates.

children.name: Sicarius terrosus
children.description: A dull-brown South American sand spider whose body texture naturally accumulates debris, providing near-perfect camouflage in sandy soils.

children.name: Sicarius patagonicus
children.description: A cold-tolerant six-eyed sand spider of Patagonian steppe; one of the most southerly members of the genus.

children.name: Sicarius fumosus
children.description: A smoky-grey South American sand spider; inhabits coastal dune fields and dry inter-Andean valleys in Argentina and Chile.

children.name: Sicarius spatulatus
children.description: A South American sand spider with unusually broad, spatula-like pedipalps; described from dry scrubland of western Argentina.

children.name: Sicarius albospinosus
children.description: A sand spider of southern Africa with distinctive white spines on the legs. Shares the same venomous capabilities and sand-burrowing habits as S. hahni.

children.name: Sicarius terratelus
children.description: A smaller Sicarius endemic to the coastal dunes of the Namib Desert. Its pale colouration provides near-perfect camouflage against the pale quartz sand of the Namib.

children.name: Sicarius levitatus
children.description: A South American Sicarius found in the arid coastal regions of Peru and Chile. Like its African relatives, it buries in loose sand and possesses a dangerously necrotic venom.

children.name: Sicarius nigrifemoratus
children.description: A sand spider from the Atacama Desert region of Chile. Its black legs contrast sharply with the pale sandy colour of the body.

children.name: Sicarius rufipes
children.description: A South American sand spider found in the dry valleys of Bolivia and Argentina. Named for its reddish leg colouration.

children.name: Sicarius lanuginosus
children.description: Named for the dense covering of fine hairs that gives the body a woolly appearance. Found in the dry inter-Andean valleys of Peru.

children.name: Sicarius allocatus
children.description: A South American sand spider from the arid Patagonian steppe of Argentina. Its pale grey colouration provides excellent camouflage in the rocky, sandy landscape.

children.name: Sicarius crassus
children.description: A heavily built Sicarius from the coastal dunes of northern Chile. Its robust pedipalps and strong legs are adapted for digging in compacted coastal sand.

children.name: Sicarius ups
children.description: A small Sicarius from the Namib-like coastal fog deserts of Peru's Paracas Peninsula, where it subsists on insects trapped in the dense coastal fog banks.

children.name: Sicarius dipper
children.description: A sand spider of the Peruvian Andes.

children.name: Sicarius dolichoscelus
children.description: A sand spider with notably long, slender legs, from Chile.

children.name: Sicarius fugata
children.description: A fast-running sand spider of the Atacama coastal ranges.

children.name: Sicarius gaspari
children.description: A sand spider of the Peruvian highlands.

children.name: Sicarius gravesi
children.description: A sand spider of the Chilean coastal fog desert.

children.name: Sicarius mapocho
children.description: A sand spider of central Chile, named after the Mapocho River.

children.name: Sicarius parvus
children.description: A tiny sand spider of the Peruvian coastal deserts.

children.name: Sicarius porcus
children.description: A stout sand spider found in coastal dunes of northern Chile.

children.name: Sicarius sordidus
children.description: A plain, sandy-coloured spider of the Patagonian steppe.

children.name: Sicarius testaceus
children.description: A brick-red sand spider of the Peruvian coast.

children.name: Sicarius andinus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius atacamensis
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius bolivianus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius choco
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius concolor
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius crustosus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius diadorus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius formalis
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius incisus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius kuntur
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius lacustris
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius lanus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius liberator
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius longipes
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius manzano
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius montanus
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius nebula
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Sicarius palustris
children.description: A venomous species with the characteristic appearance and habits of its genus. It is primarily nocturnal and hunts by ambush.

children.name: Hexophthalma hahni
children.description: The classic southern African six-eyed sand spider, formerly placed in Sicarius; ambushes prey buried under desert sand. Highly cytotoxic venom but bites on humans are virtually unknown.

children.name: Hexophthalma albospinosa
children.description: A southern African sand spider of arid regions, distinguished by pale spines; buries itself in loose substrate to ambush prey.

children.name: Hexophthalma damarensis
children.description: A six-eyed sand spider of Namibia's Damaraland; an extreme desert specialist that flicks sand grains over its body to vanish from sight.

children.name: Hexophthalma leroyi
children.description: A southern African sand spider named in honour of arachnologist John Leroy; inhabits dry sandy soils.

children.name: Hexophthalma goanikontesensis
children.description: A Namib Desert six-eyed sand spider named for the Goanikontes locality, illustrating the genus's restricted desert ranges.

children.name: Hexophthalma dolichocephalus
children.description: An African sand spider named for its elongated cephalothorax; found in arid sandy habitats of southern and southwestern Africa.

children.name: Hexophthalma elegans
children.description: An elegantly proportioned African six-eyed sand spider of Namibian and South African desert margins; a passive ambush predator.

children.name: Hexophthalma inornatus
children.description: An unadorned, pale-beige African sand spider occurring in dry sandy substrates; relies on its cryptic colouring and debris-gathering for camouflage.

children.name: Hexophthalma lawrencei
children.description: A southern African sand spider named after arachnologist R. F. Lawrence; inhabits Karoo and Kalahari sandy plains.

children.name: Hexophthalma pachycephalus
children.description: An African sand spider with a notably broad cephalothorax; recorded from dry sandy biotopes of sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Hexophthalma testaceus
children.description: A reddish-brown African six-eyed sand spider of Namibia and South Africa; uses a thin sand coat to blend with red Kalahari soils.

children.name: Hexophthalma kreyseli
children.description: A southern African desert sand spider named for collector H. Kreysel; found in Namib and Karoo arid zones.
